Glossary:
# Glossary — Computer Systems

> A quick-reference for terminology used throughout the Computer Systems curriculum. Each term links to the note where it's explained in depth.

## A

**ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit) — The part of the CPU that performs integer arithmetic and logic operations. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**AMAT** (Average Memory Access Time) — `hit_time + miss_rate × miss_penalty`. The expected time for a memory access given cache hierarchy. [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**API** (Application Programming Interface) — A contract between software components. [[Hardware/09 — DMA & Bus Mastering]]

**ASLR** (Address Space Layout Randomization) — Security technique that randomizes the base address of the stack, heap, and libraries. [[Hardware/16 — Hardware Security]]

## B

**BIOS** — Basic Input/Output System, the legacy firmware interface for x86 PCs. [[Hardware/11 — Boot Process — BIOS, UEFI & Bootloader]]

**Branch Predictor** — Hardware that guesses whether a conditional branch will be taken. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Bus** — A communication system that transfers data between components. [[Hardware/09 — DMA & Bus Mastering]]

## C

**Cache** — Small, fast memory close to the CPU that stores copies of frequently-accessed data. [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**Cache Coherence** — The protocol that ensures all caches see a consistent view of memory. [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**Cache Line** — The unit of data transfer in a cache (typically 64 bytes). [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**Calling Convention** — The rules for how functions receive arguments and return values. [[System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics]]

**CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) — CPU design philosophy with many complex instructions (e.g., x86). [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Context Switch** — Saving the state of one process/thread and restoring another. [[Concurrency/01 — Processes & Threads]]

**CPI** (Cycles Per Instruction) — Average clock cycles per instruction. A measure of CPU efficiency. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

## D

**DMA** (Direct Memory Access) — Hardware that transfers data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement. [[Hardware/09 — DMA & Bus Mastering]]

**DRAM** (Dynamic RAM) — Memory that stores each bit as a capacitor charge; needs periodic refresh. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/01 — DRAM Cells & Memory Physics]]

## E

**Endianness** — The byte order of multi-byte values: little-endian (LSB first) or big-endian (MSB first). [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness]]

## F

**False Sharing** — Two threads modify different variables that happen to share a cache line, causing unnecessary coherence traffic. [[Concurrency/07 — Synchronization Patterns]]

**Fragmentation** — Wasted memory: internal (within allocated blocks) or external (between blocks). [[Hardware/06 — Memory Allocation]]

**Frame** (Stack Frame) — The region of the stack belonging to a single function call. [[System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics]]

## G

**GPU** (Graphics Processing Unit) — A massively parallel processor optimized for graphics and compute workloads. [[Hardware/12 — GPU Architecture]]

## H

**Huge Page** — A memory page larger than 4 KB (typically 2 MB or 1 GB), reducing TLB pressure. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]]

## I

**Interrupt** — A signal to the CPU that requires immediate attention. [[Hardware/13 — Interrupts & Exceptions]]

**IPC** (Instructions Per Cycle) — Inverse of CPI; how many instructions complete per clock cycle. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**ISA** (Instruction Set Architecture) — The abstract interface between software and hardware (e.g., x86-64, ARM, RISC-V). [[Hardware/03 — Assembly Language]]

## K

**Kernel** — The core of the operating system running in privileged mode. [[System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]

## L

**Little-Endian** — Byte order where the least significant byte is stored first. Used by x86. [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness]]

## M

**MMU** (Memory Management Unit) — Hardware that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses. [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]]

**mmap** — Unix system call that maps files or devices into memory. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]]

**Moore's Law** — The observation that transistor density doubles roughly every 2 years. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Mutex** (Mutual Exclusion) — A synchronization primitive that ensures only one thread accesses a resource. [[Concurrency/04 — Data Races & Mutexes]]

## N

**NUMA** (Non-Uniform Memory Access) — Memory architecture where access latency depends on which CPU socket the memory is attached to. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]]

**NVMe** (Non-Volatile Memory Express) — A high-performance protocol for SSDs over PCIe. [[Hardware/Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]]

## O

**Out-of-Order Execution** — The ability of a CPU to execute instructions in non-sequential order to maximize throughput. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

## P

**Page** — A fixed-size block of virtual memory (typically 4 KB). [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]]

**Page Fault** — An exception raised when a virtual address has no physical mapping. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]]

**Page Table** — A data structure that maps virtual pages to physical frames. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/02 — Page Tables & TLB]]

**PCIe** (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) — High-speed serial bus standard. [[Hardware/Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]]

**Pipeline** — A CPU design where instruction processing is divided into stages, allowing multiple instructions to overlap. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Process** — An instance of a running program with its own virtual address space. [[Concurrency/01 — Processes & Threads]]

**Protection Ring** — A privilege level for code execution (Ring 0 = kernel, Ring 3 = user). [[System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]

## R

**RAID** (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) — Combining multiple drives for performance or reliability. [[Hardware/08 — RAID]]

**RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) — CPU design with simple, uniform instructions (e.g., ARM, RISC-V). [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

## S

**SATA** (Serial ATA) — Interface standard for connecting storage devices. [[Hardware/Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]]

**SIMD** (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) — Processing multiple data elements with one instruction. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**SMT** (Simultaneous Multithreading) — Running multiple threads on a single CPU core (Intel Hyper-Threading). [[Concurrency/03 — SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools]]

**Stack** — A LIFO memory region used for function calls, local variables, and return addresses. [[System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics]]

**Syscall** — A request from user space to the kernel for a privileged operation. [[System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]

## T

**TLB** (Translation Lookaside Buffer) — A cache for virtual-to-physical address translations. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/02 — Page Tables & TLB]]

**Thread** — A lightweight unit of execution that shares its address space with sibling threads. [[Concurrency/01 — Processes & Threads]]

**Transistor** — A semiconductor switch; the fundamental building block of digital circuits. [[Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]]

**Two's Complement** — The standard representation for signed integers where the most significant bit has negative weight. [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness]]

## U

**UEFI** — Unified Extensible Firmware Interface, the modern replacement for BIOS. [[Hardware/11 — Boot Process — BIOS, UEFI & Bootloader]]

## V

**Virtual Memory** — An abstraction that gives each process its own address space, backed by physical RAM and disk. [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]]

## Exercises

1. Pick 3 unfamiliar terms from this glossary. For each, read the linked note, then explain the term to an imaginary colleague in your own words.

2. **Categorization:** Group 10 terms into these categories: CPU, Memory, Concurrency, I/O. Some terms belong in multiple categories.

3. **Term chain:** Choose two terms and explain how they relate. Example: "TLB is a cache for page table entries — without it, every memory access would require walking the page table." Write 3 such chains.

Learning Path \u2014 Computer Systems:
# Computer Systems — Learning Path

> This is the recommended reading order. Each note builds on the ones before it. Notes marked "Deep Dive" are worth reading after the primary sequence.

## Foundation

Start with the big picture — how a transistor becomes a running program — then use the Glossary as your reference companion throughout.

| # | Note | Quiz | Routing |
|---|------|------|---------|
| — | [[Glossary|Glossary]] | — | Reference — consult as needed |

---

## Hardware (01–16)

From a single transistor holding charge to a CPU executing billions of instructions per second. You'll learn: how bits and bytes store numbers in real capacitors, two's complement and endianness (why 0xFF is -1 or 255 depending on interpretation), assembly language (the CPU's native tongue — MOV, ADD, CMP, JMP), CPU architecture (pipeline stages, superscalar execution, branch prediction, out-of-order execution), memory hierarchy (registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → SSD — each level 10-100× slower than the last), and memory allocation basics. After this section you understand what your code actually runs on.

| # | Note | Quiz | Routing |
|---|------|------|---------|
| 01 | [[Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems|Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] | ✓ | → 02 Negative Numbers |
| 02 | [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness|Negative Numbers & Endianness]] | ✓ | → 03 Assembly Language |
| 03 | [[Hardware/03 — Assembly Language|Assembly Language]] | ✓ | → 04 CPU Architecture |
| 04 | [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture|CPU Architecture]] | ✓ | → 05 Memory Hierarchy |
| 05 | [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy|Memory Hierarchy]] | ✓ | → 06 Memory Allocation |

---

## Memory & Storage

The memory subsystem in depth: how malloc/free actually work (free lists, coalescing, fragmentation), virtual memory (the greatest lie in computing — every process thinks it owns all RAM), page tables and TLBs (hardware caching of virtual→physical translations), page faults and mmap (demand paging, copy-on-write, memory-mapped files), huge pages and NUMA (2 MB pages, multi-socket awareness), then storage: RAID (striping, mirroring, parity), HDD mechanics (spinning rust, seek time, rotational latency), NAND flash and SSDs (wear leveling, garbage collection, write amplification), NVMe and PCIe (the 64K-deep queue that makes SSDs fast), and filesystems & the page cache (inodes, journaling, how the OS caches disk in RAM). After this section you understand why `vector.reserve()` matters and why a cache miss costs 400 cycles.

## I/O & Peripherals

How data moves between the CPU and the outside world without the CPU doing all the work. DMA (direct memory access — peripherals write to RAM directly, CPU just gets an interrupt when done), networking (TCP, UDP, sockets — the Internet's plumbing from Berkeley sockets to game netcodes), plus networking deep dives (IP, subnets, routing, TCP congestion control, UDP game protocols, TLS, game networking architecture, the boot process from BIOS/UEFI through bootloader to kernel, GPU architecture (SIMD, warps, memory bandwidth — why a GPU has 10,000 cores but each is simple), and interrupts & exceptions (how the CPU stops what it's doing to handle a keypress or a page fault without polling). After this section you understand the full I/O path from network cable to game frame.

## Virtualization & Security

How one physical machine runs many operating systems simultaneously. Hypervisors (Type 1 bare-metal vs Type 2 hosted), CPU virtualization extensions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V), memory virtualization (nested page tables — EPT/NPT), I/O virtualization (SR-IOV, device pass-through), containers vs VMs (shared kernel vs full OS isolation). Then kernel modules & drivers (how the kernel extends itself at runtime without rebooting) and hardware security (Spectre, Meltdown, side channels, TPM, Secure Boot, SGX enclaves). After this section you understand what a "cloud server" actually is at the hardware level.

| # | Note | Quiz | Routing |
|---|------|------|---------|
| 14 | [[Hardware/14 — Virtualization & Containers|Virtualization & Containers]] | ✓ | → 15 Kernel Modules |
| 15 | [[Hardware/15 — Kernel Modules & Drivers|Kernel Modules & Drivers]] | ✓ | → 16 Hardware Security |
| 16 | [[Hardware/16 — Hardware Security|Hardware Security]] | ✓ | → System Software |

---

## System Software

The software layer between hardware and your program. The compiler pipeline (preprocessor → frontend → optimizer → backend → assembler → linker — what happens at each stage), linking and executables (static vs dynamic linking, symbol resolution, PLT/GOT, position-independent code, ELF/PE formats), syscalls and the kernel (how `read()` transitions from user space to kernel space via `syscall` instruction — ring 3 to ring 0 in ~100 cycles). Then: system-level I/O (file descriptors, `open`/`read`/`write`/`close`, buffering, I/O multiplexing with `epoll`), performance optimization (CPE measurement, systematic loop optimization, latency/throughput bounds, profiling with `perf` and VTune, Amdahl's Law), signals & process control (signal handling, `sigaction`, `setjmp`/`longjmp`, `strace` and `/proc` tools), and memory bugs & garbage collection (use-after-free, buffer overflows, ASan/Valgrind, mark-and-sweep, reference counting). Then the deep dive into function calls (hardware mechanics of `call`/`ret`, calling conventions, stack frame layout, buffer overflow vulnerabilities, stack canaries, ASLR). After this section you understand the invisible machinery between `main()` and the CPU — and how to make it fast, correct, and debuggable.

## Concurrency

The hardest problem in systems programming: multiple things happening at once. Processes vs threads (separate address spaces vs shared heap), the OS scheduler (CFS, MLFQ, proportional share — how the kernel decides who runs next), SMT/hyperthreading and thread pools (hardware threads, affinity, work stealing), data races and mutexes (the fundamental concurrency bug + the fundamental fix), deadlock (the four conditions, lock ordering, `std::lock`), atomics and memory ordering (lock-free programming, acquire/release semantics, the CPU's store buffer), synchronization patterns (producer-consumer, read-write locks, barriers, semaphores), lock-free programming (CAS loops, ABA problem, hazard pointers), and practical concurrency (how game engines structure their thread architecture — main thread, render thread, worker threads, async I/O). After this section you can reason about multi-threaded code from the CPU's memory model up.

Learning Path \u2014 C++:
# C++ — Learning Path

## Phase 1 — Primer (01 — Primer/)

Absolute beginner entry point. You'll learn what a variable IS at the hardware level — capacitors holding charge mapped to names via the compiler — not just `int x = 5`. Covers the stack (RSP subtract = allocation, one CPU instruction), control flow (how `if`/`for` change RIP), functions (call/ret, stack frames, calling conventions), arrays in contiguous memory, pointers as typed addresses, and the full compile→link→run pipeline. After this phase you can read any basic C++ program and trace its execution from source text to CPU instructions. No quizzes here — just type every example and complete every exercise.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 01 | [[01 — Primer/01 — Variables, Types & Memory|Variables, Types & Memory]] | ✓ |
| 02 | [[01 — Primer/02 — Control Flow|Control Flow]] | ✓ |
| 03 | [[01 — Primer/03 — Functions & The Call Stack|Functions & The Call Stack]] | ✓ |
| 04 | [[01 — Primer/04 — Arrays, Vectors & Loops|Arrays, Vectors & Loops]] | ✓ |
| 05 | [[01 — Primer/05 — Pointers & References|Pointers & References]] | ✓ |
| 06 | [[01 — Primer/06 — Compiling & Running Programs|Compiling & Running Programs]] | ✓ |


## Phase 2 — Syntax & Types (02 — Syntax & Types/)

C++-specific syntax that doesn't exist in C or other languages. Enums and enum class (why the C++11 version is strictly safer), references as non-nullable aliases, the many meanings of `static` (file-local, class-scope, thread-local, function-persistent), structured bindings for destructuring tuples and structs, type deduction rules (`auto`, `decltype`, `decltype(auto)`), implicit vs explicit conversions and the `explicit` keyword, const correctness (the most important keyword in C++ for preventing bugs), the preprocessor and macros (and why modern C++ replaces most of them), string types (`std::string`, `std::string_view`, C-strings, SSO), and `std::format` (type-safe, locale-aware replacement for `printf` and string streams). After this phase you can read any C++ syntax with confidence.

## Phase 3 — Memory (03 — Memory/)

C++'s defining feature: you control where every object lives. Stack vs heap (one instruction vs ~50-200+ cycles), object memory layout (vtable pointers, padding, alignment, cache-line effects), smart pointers (`unique_ptr` = exclusive ownership with zero overhead, `shared_ptr` = atomic reference counting with control blocks, `weak_ptr` = cycle-breaking observer), RAII (the single most important C++ idiom — constructors acquire, destructors release, exceptions can't leak), `std::array` vs C arrays (bounds safety, `.size()`, no decay to pointer), `std::vector` internals (capacity doubling, amortized O(1), iterator invalidation, SSO for strings), `std::map`/`unordered_map` (red-black tree vs hash table, key requirements, collision resolution), iterators and algorithms (the STL's separation of data from operations), `std::span` (non-owning view over contiguous data), `std::filesystem` (portable path manipulation), and the full container ecosystem (`deque`, `set`, `stack`, `queue`, `priority_queue`, `std::pmr`). After this phase you understand how memory actually works in C++ — not just what to type, but what happens at the silicon level.

## Phase 4 — OOP & Generics (04 — OOP & Generics/)

The two pillars of C++ abstraction: runtime polymorphism (inheritance + virtual functions) and compile-time polymorphism (templates). Inheritance and virtual dispatch (vtables, vptr, the cost of a virtual call vs static dispatch), lambdas and function objects (closures, captures by value/reference, `std::function`'s type-erasure cost), C++ casting (`static_cast`, `dynamic_cast`, `const_cast`, `reinterpret_cast` — when each is correct and when each is a bug) plus RTTI, operator overloading (making your types feel like built-ins — but don't abuse it), and templates + concepts (compile-time code generation, SFINAE, fold expressions, C++20 concepts as type constraints). After this phase you can design abstractions that are both type-safe and zero-overhead.

| 06 | [[04 — OOP & Generics/06 — Multiple Inheritance|Multiple Inheritance]] | ✓ |


## Phase 5 — Modern C++ (05 — Modern C++/)

C++11/14/17/20/23 features that change how you write code. Move semantics and perfect forwarding (rvalue references, `std::move`, `std::forward`, the rule of five vs rule of zero), `constexpr`/`consteval`/`constinit` (moving computation to compile time — faster runtime, caught-at-build errors), `noexcept` and its interaction with move semantics (why vector grows by copying when moves aren't noexcept), sum types (`std::variant` replaces unions, `std::optional` replaces sentinel values, `std::any` for true type erasure), coroutines (stackless, suspendable functions — the foundation of C++20 async), `std::ranges` (composable, lazy, pipeable algorithms — no more `begin()`/`end()` boilerplate), and the C++23 overview (deducing `this`, `std::expected`, monadic operations, `if consteval`). After this phase you write C++ the way it's meant to be written today, not 1998.

| 08 | [[05 — Modern C++/08 — Value Categories|Value Categories]] | ✓ |


## Phase 6 — Bridge to Unreal Engine (06 — Bridge/)

Connecting standard C++ to Unreal Engine's C++ dialect. C++ vs UE (why UE has its own containers, macros, GC, and reflection — the design constraints of a real-time game engine), the full compile pipeline (preprocessor → compiler → assembler → linker — for both standard C++ and UE's UHT pass), C++20 modules (replacing `#include` with pre-compiled module interfaces — dramatically faster builds), exceptions and error handling (why UE disables exceptions and uses `ensure()`/`check()`/`verify()` instead), and CMake & build systems (how your `.cpp` files become an executable — targets, dependencies, toolchains, and why UE uses UBT instead). After this phase you can work in any C++ codebase, engine or not.

Learning Path \u2014 Game Math:
# Game Math — Learning Path

## Foundation

The three pillars of 3D math. Trigonometry & Vectors (dot product = projection, cross product = perpendicular vector — the two operations that power all of 3D graphics), Matrices & Coordinate Systems (4×4 homogeneous matrices, column-major storage, model→world→view→clip transforms — how a 3D point travels from artist's model to your screen), and Quaternions & 3D Rotations (4D math that avoids gimbal lock, interpolates smoothly via slerp, and composes without matrix multiplication). Start with the full-stack overview note (`From Trigonometry to 3D Transformations`) to see how these connect, then dive into each. After this section you can place, rotate, and project any object in 3D space.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[From Trigonometry to 3D Transformations|From Trig to 3D — Full Picture]] | — |
| 1 | [[Trigonometry & Vectors|Trigonometry & Vectors]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[Matrices & Coordinate Systems|Matrices & Coordinate Systems]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Quaternions & 3D Rotations|Quaternions & 3D Rotations]] | ✓ |


## Physics

Making objects move and collide realistically. Rigid body dynamics (F=ma, torque, inertia tensors — the Newtonian mechanics behind every physics engine), numerical integration (Euler, semi-implicit Euler, Verlet, RK4 — how to advance a simulation forward in time without it exploding), constraints & contact (joints, resting contact, impulse-based resolution, Baumgarte stabilization), and continuous collision detection (preventing fast-moving objects from tunneling through walls — swept volumes, time-of-impact calculation). Start with the Collision Detection Overview for the big picture.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[Physics/Collision Detection/00 — Collision Detection Overview|Collision Detection Overview]] | — |
| — | [[Physics/Simulation/01 — Rigid Body Dynamics|Rigid Body Dynamics]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Physics/Simulation/02 — Numerical Integration|Numerical Integration]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Physics/Simulation/03 — Constraints & Contact|Constraints & Contact]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Physics/Simulation/04 — Continuous Collision Detection|Continuous Collision Detection]] | ✓ |


## Collision Detection

Determining whether two shapes overlap — the computational core of any physics engine. AABB and sphere tests (the fast, cheap primitives — axis-aligned boxes and radial checks), ray casting (shooting a line through space and finding what it hits first — used for weapon traces, visibility, and picking), Separating Axis Theorem (the workhorse of convex collision — project onto candidate axes, check for gaps), and GJK/EPA (the general algorithm for arbitrary convex shapes — used by every major physics engine). After this section you can detect and resolve collisions between any convex shapes.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[Physics/Collision Detection/01 — AABB and Sphere|AABB & Sphere]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Physics/Collision Detection/02 — Ray Casting|Ray Casting]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Physics/Collision Detection/03 — Separating Axis Theorem|Separating Axis Theorem]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Physics/Collision Detection/04 — GJK Algorithm|GJK Algorithm]] | ✓ |

*Note: Collision Detection is listed under Physics in the vault folder structure.*


## Animation

Making characters move. Interpolation & Bezier splines (lerp, smoothstep, cubic Bezier, Catmull-Rom — the curves that define motion paths and animation blending), skeletal animation (bones, skinning matrices, inverse kinematics — how a character's mesh deforms when its skeleton moves), slerp & kinematics (spherical linear interpolation for quaternion blending, forward and inverse kinematics for joint chains). Start with the Curves Overview.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[Animation/Curves & Interpolation/00 — Curves Overview|Curves Overview]] | — |
| — | [[Animation/Curves & Interpolation/01 — Interpolation, Bezier & Splines|Interpolation, Bezier & Splines]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Animation/Skeletal Animation/01 — Skeletal Animation|Skeletal Animation]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Animation/Skeletal Animation/02 — Slerp & Kinematics|Slerp & Kinematics]] | ✓ |


## Navigation

How characters find their way through a 3D world. A* pathfinding (the classic graph search — heuristic-guided, guaranteed optimal for admissible heuristics), steering behaviors (seek, flee, arrive, wander, flocking — emergent movement without explicit paths), and navmesh generation (automatically building walkable surfaces from level geometry — Recast/Detour internals). After this section you can make AI characters navigate any environment.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[Navigation/01 — A Star & Pathfinding|A* & Pathfinding]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Navigation/02 — Steering Behaviors|Steering Behaviors]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Navigation/03 — Navmesh Generation|Navmesh Generation]] | ✓ |


## Rendering

How 3D math becomes pixels on screen. Projection & clip space (perspective divide, near/far planes, NDC — the matrix that makes distant things smaller), barycentric coordinates (interpolating across triangles — the math behind every pixel shader), BRDF & lighting models (how light bounces off surfaces — diffuse, specular, Fresnel, PBR), spherical harmonics (compressing lighting into coefficients — ambient light probes, diffuse irradiance), Monte Carlo & path tracing (random sampling to solve the rendering equation — the math behind Cycles, Arnold, and offline rendering), color spaces & HDR (sRGB vs linear, tone mapping, gamut), signed distance fields (implicit surfaces, smooth blending, ray marching), and voxels & octrees (volumetric data structures — Minecraft to medical imaging). After this section you understand what happens between `DrawPrimitive()` and the pixel on your monitor.

## Procedural

Generating content algorithmically instead of by hand. Noise generation (Perlin, simplex, Worley — the building blocks of procedural terrain, clouds, and textures), sampling & placement (Poisson disc, blue noise, stratified sampling — placing objects without clumping), L-systems & marching cubes (grammar-based plant generation, isosurface extraction for voxel terrain), and probability & randomness (distributions, expected value, Monte Carlo estimation — the statistical foundations). After this section you can generate infinite worlds.

| — | [[Procedural/04 — Probability & Randomness|Probability & Randomness]] | ✓ |


## Audio & Networking Math

Math that bridges disciplines. Frequency domain & FFT (converting time→frequency, spectral analysis, convolution — the math behind audio processing and compression), spatial audio (HRTF, attenuation, Doppler effect — how sound behaves in 3D space), and network interpolation (dead reckoning, extrapolation, smoothing — the math that hides network latency in multiplayer games).

| — | [[Audio & Networking/02 — Spatial Audio|Spatial Audio]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Audio & Networking/03 — Network Interpolation|Network Interpolation]] | ✓ |


## Geometric Algebra — The Capstone

A unified mathematical framework that subsumes vectors, quaternions, complex numbers, and projective geometry into a single algebra. Multivectors, products & rotors (geometric product, wedge product, bivectors, trivectors — rotation as a single operation, not matrix + quaternion + Euler angle), and PGA & applications (Projective Geometric Algebra — points, lines, and planes as algebraic elements; translations and rotations as the same operation). This is the deep end — read after mastering vectors, matrices, and quaternions. Start with the overview.

| — | [[Geometric Algebra/02 — PGA & Applications|PGA & Applications]] | ✓ |


## Engine Bridge — UE Math

Connecting pure math to Unreal Engine's implementation. FVector/FRotator/FQuat (UE's core math types — how they differ from pure Vec3/Quat, when to use each), FTransform & FMatrix (UE's 4×4 transform — scale→rotate→translate composition), FMath (interpolation, utility functions, the kitchen sink of UE math), collision & intersection math (UE-specific collision primitives and tests), and curves/ranges/random (UE's curve editor, FRichCurve, random number generation). After this section you can use UE's math libraries fluently, knowing what's engine convenience vs what's pure math.

Learning Path \u2014 Unreal Engine Core:
# UE Core — Learning Path

Start with the full-stack overview (`From UCLASS to Running Object`) to see how UHT, reflection, GC, and the object system connect before diving into each piece.

## Core Systems

The foundation every UE class sits on. UHT, macros & reflection (how `UCLASS()`/`UPROPERTY()`/`UFUNCTION()` feed the Unreal Header Tool to generate the reflection system — the hidden `.generated.h` layer that makes serialization, GC, and Blueprint exposure work), UE inheritance & UObject system (the class hierarchy from UObject through UActorComponent to AActor — why everything inherits from UObject and what you get for free), pointer ecosystem (TObjectPtr, TSharedPtr, TWeakObjectPtr, TSoftObjectPtr — when to use each, what the GC tracks, and what you must manage yourself), and FName/FString/FText (three string types for three different jobs — identifiers, mutable text, and localized display strings). After this section you understand UE's object model from the ground up.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[From UCLASS to Running Object — The UE Core Pipeline|Full-Stack Pipeline]] | — |
| 1 | [[UHT, Macros & Reflection|UHT, Macros & Reflection]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[UE Inheritance & UObject System|UE Inheritance & UObject System]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Pointer Ecosystem|Pointer Ecosystem]] | ✓ |
| 4 | [[FName vs FString vs FText|FName vs FString vs FText]] | ✓ |


## Containers

UE's custom container library — why it exists, how it differs from the STL, and when to use each. TArray internals (the GC-tracked, UPROPERTY-compatible dynamic array built on FMemory::Realloc instead of operator new), TMap internals (UE's hash map — built on TSparseArray with separate key/value arrays and a hash table for O(1) lookup), TSet internals (TMap without values — fast membership testing with the same sparse-array backing), and UE templates & T-containers (why UE uses its own containers instead of std::vector/std::map — reflection, serialization, network replication, and GC integration). After this section you can choose the right container for any UE task and understand the performance implications.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | [[TArray Internals|TArray Internals]] | ✓ |
| 6 | [[TMap Internals|TMap Internals]] | ✓ |
| 7 | [[TSet Internals|TSet Internals]] | ✓ |
| 8 | [[UE Templates & T-Containers|UE Templates & T-Containers]] | ✓ |


## Casting & Types

Type conversion in UE's reflection system. Casting overview (why `Cast<T>()` replaces `dynamic_cast` — 1-2 cycles via bitwise flag check instead of 50-200+ cycles via vtable walk), cast internals (EClassCastFlags, the UClass hierarchy, and the macro-generated casting support), interface casting (how UE's `IIterface` system works with multiple inheritance without the diamond problem), and pitfalls (when Cast returns nullptr silently, when it crashes, and the static cast alternatives). After this section you can safely navigate UE's type system at runtime.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[Casting/00 — Casting Overview|Casting Overview]] | — |
| — | [[Casting/01 — Cast Internals & Variants|Cast Internals & Variants]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Casting/02 — Interface Casting & References|Interface Casting & References]] | ✓ |
| — | [[Casting/03 — When to Use & Pitfalls|When to Use & Pitfalls]] | ✓ |


## Advanced

Edge cases and modern patterns. Move semantics in UE (how UE handles move construction with its own types — TArray, FString, TUniquePtr — and why you can't use std::move on UObject pointers), and UE enums & flags (UENUM, EClassCastFlags as bitfield enums, and how UE's enum system integrates with Blueprints and serialization). After this section you can write idiomatic, performant UE C++ that plays nicely with the engine's expectations.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 9 | [[Move Semantics in UE|Move Semantics in UE]] | ✓ |
| 10 | [[UE Enums & Flags|UE Enums & Flags]] | ✓ |
Learning Path \u2014 Unreal Engine Netwo:
# UE Networking — Learning Path

Start with the full-stack overview (`From Input to Replication`) to see how an input travels from client to server and back before diving into each piece.

## Foundation

The client-server model in Unreal Engine. Network roles (Authority, Autonomous Proxy, Simulated Proxy — who owns what and who can modify what), RPCs (Server/Client/NetMulticast — calling functions across the network with UFUNCTION metadata), RepNotifies & OnRep (automatic property replication with callback hooks — the standard way to sync state), and PushModel & dirty marking (UE5's optimization — only replicate properties that actually changed, not the whole object every frame). After this section you can build a basic multiplayer game with synced state.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| — | [[From Input to Replication — The Networking Pipeline|Full-Stack Pipeline]] | — |
| 1 | [[Network Roles|Network Roles]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[RPCs in Unreal Engine|RPCs in Unreal Engine]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[RepNotifies & OnRep|RepNotifies & OnRep]] | ✓ |
| 4 | [[PushModel & Dirty Marking|PushModel & Dirty Marking]] | ✓ |


## Iris — UE5's New Replication System

UE5's ground-up rewrite of the replication system. Iris overview (why Epic replaced the legacy replication — better CPU cache behavior, data-oriented design, per-object filtering at the serialization level, and configurable replication graphs), and Iris filtering & prioritization (how to control which objects replicate to which clients — spatial filtering, frequency-based prioritization, custom conditions). After this section you understand the modern UE5 replication pipeline and can optimize it for your game's needs.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | [[Iris Replication System Overview|Iris Replication System Overview]] | ✓ |
| 6 | [[Iris Filtering & Prioritization|Iris Filtering & Prioritization]] | ✓ |


## Advanced

The deepest layer of UE networking. GAS networking & prediction (how the Gameplay Ability System handles networked abilities — client prediction, server reconciliation, rollback for mistakes — the same techniques used by Fortnite and every UE-based multiplayer game). After this section you can build a competitive multiplayer game with server-authoritative abilities and client-side prediction that feels responsive.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | [[GAS Networking & Prediction|GAS Networking & Prediction]] | ✓ |
Learning Path \u2014 Algorithms & Comple:
# Algorithms & Complexity — Learning Path

## Foundations

The language of algorithmic analysis. The Big O complexity spectrum (O(1) → O(log n) → O(n) → O(n log n) → O(n²) → O(2ⁿ) — what each means, how to recognize them in real code, and why the difference between O(n²) and O(n log n) is the difference between "runs in 1 second" and "runs in 30 years"), amortized analysis & real profiling (why `vector::push_back` is O(1) amortized — geometric growth means each element is copied at most twice on average — and how to profile real C++ code to verify theoretical complexity with `perf` and timing), and applied examples comparing raw C++ vs Unreal Engine (the same algorithm in std::vector vs TArray, std::sort vs TArray::Sort — theoretical complexity is the same, but constant factors differ dramatically due to allocators, cache behavior, and engine overhead). After this section you can read any algorithm and tell whether it will scale.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum|The Big O Complexity Spectrum]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data|Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Applied Examples - Raw C++ vs Unreal Engine|Applied Examples — C++ vs UE]] | ✓ |

## Classic Algorithms

Three algorithm families every game developer encounters. Sorting algorithms (bubble, insertion, merge, quick, heap, intro — why std::sort uses Introsort, a hybrid of quicksort + heapsort + insertion sort, and when each is cache-friendly), graph algorithms (BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, A* — from pathfinding to dependency resolution, the algorithms that power navigation, build systems, and social graphs), and dynamic programming (memoization, tabulation, optimal substructure — solving hard problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, from inventory systems to animation blending). After this section you have a working toolkit of classic algorithms and know when each applies.

DeepVault Guide: # DeepVault Guide Welcome to DeepVault — an interactive learning fortress for mastering C++, Unreal Engine, and the computer systems beneath them. ## How This Works Every folder on the dashboard contains **Learn** notes and **Quiz** exercises: | Mode | What It Does | |---|---| | **Learn** | The full vault note, rendered exactly as it appears in Obsidian. Read, scroll the right-side table of contents, click wiki links to jump between notes. | | **Quiz** | 5-10 randomized questions per note — multiple choice and text input. Instant feedback. Scores saved locally on your device. | ### Tracking Your Progress Progress is based on **quiz scores only** — reading a note does not count. Here's how it works: | Status | What It Means | |---|---| | **NOT STARTED** | You haven't taken the quiz yet | | **IN PROGRESS** | At least 1 attempt, but best score is below 80% | | **COMPLETED** | You scored 80% or higher on at least one attempt | The folder header shows your overall completion percentage. Quiz scores and attempt history are saved locally via localStorage — they never leave your device. ### Bookmarks **Bookmark notes** you want to return to — click the ☆ star on any note card in the Study tab. Bookmarked notes appear under the ⭐ Bookmarks tab for quick access. - Click ☆ to bookmark → turns to ⭐ (gold) - Click ⭐ to remove bookmark - Switch to the Bookmarks tab to see only your bookmarked notes - Bookmarks are included in Export/Import — they travel with your progress **Wiki links** like [[04 — CPU Architecture]] connect notes to each other. Click any link to jump to that note's Learn page. **Recent notes** appear on the dashboard below your folders — quick jump back to what you were studying. ### WIP Categories Categories that are still under development show an amber dot badge ● on the dashboard. Hover or long-press for "Work in progress" — these categories have fewer notes but are actively growing. --- ## Master Study Order This vault is designed to be read in order. Each note builds on the ones before it. See the [[Learning Path]] for the full reading order — this is just the big-picture map. ### Layer 1 — Computer Systems (start here) Without understanding the hardware, C++ is just magic words. Start with the physical reality. Covering: bits and bytes → assembly → CPU → memory hierarchy → virtual memory → storage → DMA → networking → boot process → GPU → interrupts → system software → concurrency. Over 50 notes from transistor to thread pool. Start with [[01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Computer Systems|Computer Systems Learning Path]]. ### Layer 2 — C++ Once you understand the hardware, learn the language that runs on it. 52 notes across 6 phases: Primer (absolute beginner) → Syntax & Types → Memory → OOP & Generics → Modern C++ → Engine Bridge. Every note has exercises, See Also, and quizzes. Start with [[01 — Primer/01 — Variables, Types & Memory|Variables, Types & Memory]] and follow the [[Learning Path — C++|C++ Learning Path]]. ### Layer 3 — Game Math The mathematics that make 3D worlds possible. 45 notes across 10 subfolders: Trigonometry → Matrices → Quaternions → Collision Detection → Physics Simulation → Animation → Navigation → Rendering → Procedural Generation → Audio & Networking → Geometric Algebra → UE Bridge. Framework-agnostic (pure C++ structs, not UE-specific). Start with [[From Trigonometry to 3D Transformations]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Game Math|Game Math Learning Path]]. ### Layer 4 — Unreal Engine Core How UE turns C++ into a game engine. 14 notes: UHT & Macros → Pointer Ecosystem → String Types → Casting → Containers → Templates → Move Semantics. Start with [[UHT, Macros & Reflection]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Unreal Engine Core|UE Core Learning Path]]. ### Layer 5 — UE Networking Multiplayer from the hardware up. 7 notes: Network Roles → RPCs → RepNotifies → PushModel → Iris Replication → Iris Filtering → GAS Networking. Start with [[Network Roles]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Unreal Engine Networking|UE Networking Learning Path]]. ### Layer 6 — Big O & Algorithms 6 notes: Complexity classes → Amortized analysis → Applied examples → Sorting algorithms → Graph algorithms → Dynamic programming. Start with [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Algorithms & Complexity|Algorithms Learning Path]]. --- ## Reference - [[Glossary]] — Every acronym defined (MMU, TLB, PTE, MESI, PLT, GOT...) - [Learning Paths](#/reference/Learning%20Path) — All six category paths - Use the right-side table of contents to jump between sections in any note - Check the dashboard for recently viewed notes and quiz scores --- DeepVault is built for Mayk's vault. All notes are source-verified against UE 5.8 engine code. ### Moving Between Devices Your quiz progress lives in your browser's localStorage. To continue studying on another device: 1. Export on your current device — downloads a JSON file 2. Transfer the file (email, AirDrop, cloud) 3. Import on the new device — merges all your scores Export and Import buttons are at the bottom of the dashboard. No account needed. ### Cloud Sync Sync your progress across all devices with a free GitHub token. Click **☁️ Sync** at the bottom of the dashboard: 1. Create a **classic token** at [github.com/settings/tokens](https://github.com/settings/tokens) — check ONLY the `gist` scope 2. Paste it into DeepVault and click **Connect** 3. Progress auto-saves to a private GitHub Gist after every quiz and bookmark 4. On another device, paste the same token — all progress auto-loads Your data stays in YOUR GitHub account — private, encrypted, invisible to anyone else. --- Double-click index.html to launch. Everything stays on your device.
